speaker: interviewer
text: Was that 1945 now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1945 gradually we heard that the war was over. And we felt now we are going back--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. Gradually? There wasn't one big announcement?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because in Wales we're a little bit away from the mainstream. And we kept in touch with the people in London that we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you one thing before you continue-- did you-- by this time, did you know what was happening to the Jews of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the unfortunate thing was that we were not allowed to correspond with any of my family not in Europe, because England was in war with Germany. And we couldn't correspond with Israel, where my two brothers and my sister was. And I could not correspond with my sister who lived in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wonder why that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They-- while all this was going-- so we felt we were cut off completely. We were alone. But we never gave up hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you read the newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We read the newspapers, and we saw the handwriting on the wall. And so then we-- my father-in-law somehow went-- he was in England, in London. And he went to America, sponsored by their son Alfred, who was already living in Chicago. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: This must be a brother of Hugo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hugo. And he was sponsored by the relatives of his wife, Rost-- Ros-- the big--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rothschild?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rothschild. Yeah. They were sponsored by them. So they got out shortly after 1937 to America. So they wanted to get their father out. So the father went from London. They got him into London and we saw him, and he was on the ship the [INAUDIBLE] to the Ellis Island. And he was sent back for some reason or other. Then they had to get more affidavits, more papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to London?

speaker: interviewee
text: In London. And then finally he got out, and that's how my brother-in-law found out where we were. And so the correspondence was lifted. We could write to him and my sister. The problem was we didn't know where they lived. You know? We had no idea. But then later on we found out they lived in San Bernardino.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then from the Wales, we went back to England and we started on our papers. And my cousin would not-- we lost the affidavit of my cousin, Jenny [? Schpiesburger, ?] because the war broke out. And that was the reason we couldn't migrate to America, because the war broke out and everything was stopped. And therefore we couldn't go. That's why we had to stay seven years in England instead of six weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so what happened was we went back and Hugo got his job back. And I didn't work then anymore, because I was working on my affidavits to go to America. And my sister, who then lived in San Bernardino and worked for Burke's Market Spot, a big produce company like Lucky's. And he was the owner and my sister asked him if he would sponsor us. And my sister sponsored us, and Mr. Burke sponsored us, and Hugo's brother gave us the affidavit. So finally, we got the affidavit and we finally prepared to come to this country in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In fact, my husband made good money and I had made money. And I had learned from my parents, when you make $1.00 you don't spend it. You keep some back. So when Hugo's cousin, he was in the American army, and found us in London. And when he went back to America, took some of the money back to America. And by the time we left England we were able to take some money back. And not only that, the Jewish-- the Jewish community, or the Jewish-- they paid for our--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Joint?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish committee in London paid our hostel expenses, where we hardly got any food. But when we left, we paid them double what they had spent on us. So we went with a clean bill. We didn't owe anyone a nickel. And we left then, and we came-- and we came to New York, where I had an aunt. And my brother-in-law had arranged for a hotel to stay for a couple of days. And my father-in-law was supposed to pick us up and my brother-in-law was supposed to pick us up from the boat in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they-- Gripsholm. But they could not come, because the train was on strike. And at that time they didn't think of flying. So they couldn't come. So my mother's sister lived in New York. She was married to an opera singer from Galatia. An opera singer and a cantor. And he had already died, but she was living still in Brooklyn. And her daughter Kathy Ginsberg, they picked us up. And when they heard that we are not going to stay with them, they were kind of disappointed. So we stayed with them and we canceled our hotel room. And it was very strange. You must understand that we lived under very, very difficult circumstance in England. We had always black outs. We didn't get the food. We had no light.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a war, after all.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a war until 1945. In fact, we went to that victory dinner on Piccadilly that they just showed very recently. We were right in that group, and celebrated the victory of the Allies and that Hitler finally got caught up and killed. Any how, so when we came to America and they took us to a very elegant place. And they brought me a steak, and I started to cut up the steak in 12 pieces. They said what are you cutting up the steak for? I said, that's for all of us. No. That's your steak! We were used to no meat period. And the big steak. When we saw so many people, I got dizzy. When we went-- they took us out to theaters and I saw so many people, I had to stop and close my eyes. I got dizzy from seeing all these people. But they were wonderful. And then we came to Chicago, and my brother had arranged for us to get a beautiful apartment. And but we had-- and he had a gorgeous, gorgeous store. A jewelry store in Chicago, in the Pittsfield building. And he felt that Hugo should work with him for a while to get acquainted with the American way of business. I didn't work then. I took care of my baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your baby, by this time, was six or seven?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was about six, seven. Yeah. And I felt that it was more important for me to take care of Sonia. And she became eight, and she became enlisted in a ballerina class.

speaker: interviewer
text: A ballet class?

speaker: interviewee
text: A ballet class, yes. And she did very well. And she danced in the Nutcracker suite, and it was very happy. And the day we arrived, we already had bought tickets in England to go from Chicago to San Bernadino to visit my sister. That was our priority, because after all she was the only surviving member-- family-- family member.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to ask you to do something that might be a little difficult. In one nice little sentence explain your life in America. Not in detail, because we've heard in wonderful detail your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just sum it up for us. And then I'll ask you a final question.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. America was a dream of our life. We had been given the opportunity to become English and British citizens, but we refused because America was our life. We wanted to be united with Hugo's family and my family. I have never been disappointed. I love America. I thank God for being here, for raising my child and my grandchildren, and to having more and more family here. And I say, thank God for America, that we have found liberty and freedom. And we are very proud, and my husband equally so. When we came to San Bernardino, we started from scratch. The city opened their arms and made us feel welcome. I became active in our city. Our mayor came right away to our store upstairs, found out that I speak German. He had lots of correspondence with German. I became his translator. Later I worked almost for every mayor on a voluntary basis. I felt they gave us a living, it's only fair for me to accept when they asked me to volunteer. And I did. I only did when they asked me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your ingenuity, getting yourself out of Germany and managing in England, you used to make a good life for yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly

speaker: interviewer
text: In this country with your husband and your child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you attribute your being-- what is the thing that drove you? So many people didn't get out of Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I lost a lot of people in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you lose?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost my father, who was killed on the transport to a concentration camp. He was murdered. I lost my mother, died of cancer in 1940, but under horrible conditions. My sister Jenny, the oldest from Berlin who married a man, Mr. Rosenbaum, was seen in a concentration camp-- I think it was Theresienstadt-- going into the gas chamber, never coming out any more. Both of them were killed. My sister, Renate, who married on the day I was having my baby, on the tenth of April. She was, she married a Polish man, was deported the next day to Poland. And they were killed after a little while most horribly in Poland. I lost a lot-- my-- an aunt and an uncle and their five children out of [INAUDIBLE] were killed and murdered in concentration camps. Never heard of again. I have lost my husband's stepmother, from Dusseldorf, and his step sister. I have all the pictures. They were transported to Poland. They were seen digging their own grave and shot. And they didn't even look whether they were dead or alive, and were buried, most likely alive. And I have lost a lot of uncles and aunts and cousins from my mother's side. I lost a lot, a lot. My mother's sister, my mother's brothers. A lot of them were all killed in concentration camps. It was horrible. It was horrible. And we-- and that is the reason why I keep my eyes and ears open so it should never ever happen again. I belong to a lot of organizations-- the World Jewish Congress, the AIPAC, the American Jewish Congress. I belong-- I was a director here of the Redevelopment Agency. I was very much involved for one reason-- you cannot live by bread alone, and you cannot only think of yourself. You have to keep your eyes and ears open for the future. The row on the left is my sister Dora, Becca, my brother Isidore Rovell, my sister Jenny Rosenbaum, who lost her life in a concentration camp. My brother Paul, who lives here. My sister Renate, who also perished in the concentration camp. Then it's me--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the first row?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first row. It's myself, my mother--

speaker: interviewer
text: State your name. My-- enemy Irmy for short, Irmgard officially, Blum. My mother, Henrietta Rowelsky. My younger, sister Lea, who lives in a kibbutz in Israel. My father Louis. And my sister Ruth, who I am still looking for. What year--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that must have been while we were still in Kaukehman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany. East Prussia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year would you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: That could be somewhere between 1930 and 1932. Somewhere around there. That is me in that fur collar, I think I was already in Berlin in the early years 1933. And above also was a picture from Berlin, my little girl Sonia and myself and my husband. This is my husband Hugo and myself, Irmy. And we were not married yet. We were engaged.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were engaged in 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin. Husband Hugo, myself, Irmy, and my little girl Sonia. And that was when we took her back with us to London. That was one of our happy, happy days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. Here I am working for the [INAUDIBLE] Millinery Company, and I'm making hats. And I am the woman with the white blouse on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about 1946-47.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In London. This was a picture taken here in San Bernardino a few years ago-- it must have been about five or six years ago-- celebrating my husband's birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would that be 1990? Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: A litte earlier-- 1988 I would say. And on the far left, the second row, is Larry, our grandson. Myself. And Alan, our grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your grandson's last-- what are your grandson's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alan-- Lavitt. Larry Levitt and Alan Levitt. And Laurie Lavitt Schmidt. And my husband, Hugo, and my daughter, Sonia. That was also one of the happy days with my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was San Bernadino?

speaker: interviewee
text: In San Bernardino, in the home where I'm at now.
